title: Practical Limits on Integrated Squeezers
authors: Devin J. Dean, Taewon Park, Lars S. Madsen, Alex Terrasson, Sam Robison, Geun Ho Ahn, Ziyu Wang, Hubert S. Stokowski, Luke Qi, Jesse J. Slim, Joel Corney, Darwin Serkland, Warwick P. Bowen, Martin M. Fejer, Amir H. Safavi-Naeini
abstract: Recent experiments have demonstrated the successful generation and detection of moderately squeezed vacuum states with integrated photonics. However, in order to benefit from the reduced noise of highly squeezed light, many different noise sources must be mitigated. Here, we quantify the fundamental limits these noise sources impose on squeezing measurements and find surprising generality across different platforms and designs. We combine these different limitations into a simple model that provides practical guidance for the design and benchmarking of next-generation integrated squeezed-light systems.
summary: This paper develops a comprehensive, unified theoretical model to determine the practical limits on generating and measuring highly squeezed light in integrated photonic circuits. It quantifies how various noise sources—including internal loss, pump saturation, and phase noise—degrade the quantum squeezing. The resulting model offers crucial design guidance for advancing integrated quantum light sources.
main_problem: The paper quantifies the fundamental limits imposed by various noise sources on measuring highly squeezed light, particularly for next-generation integrated squeezed-light systems.
main_result: A unified model is developed that combines limitations from generation (e.g., internal loss, pump saturation) and detection (e.g., phase noise, electronic noise) into a single variance budget.
method: The authors use the Gaussian quantum optics framework, deriving variance evolution equations and applying noise mixing approximations to model the cumulative effect of multiple loss and noise processes.
model_or_system: The focus is on integrated nonlinear photonic circuits used as squeezed light sources (e.g., OPA). The analysis covers nonresonant, pump-resonant, and signal-resonant squeezing regimes.
key_observables: Squeezed quadrature variance ($V_{-}$), anti-squeezed variance ($V_{+}$), and the overall achievable squeezing level (in dB).
important_parameters: Nonlinearity-to-loss ratio ($g/\alpha$), internal loss rates ($\eta_i$), external loss ($\eta_e$), and phase error ($\delta	heta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Gaussian quantum optics framework and often uses the low-loss approximation for noise summation ($1 - \eta \ll 1$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate noise sources in a generic measurement setup, the comprehensive variance budget combining multiple noise terms, and plots showing fundamental bounds on achievable squeezing versus gain rate.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the general variance budget, then deriving specific gain formulas for different squeezer types (nonresonant, signal-resonant), and finally unifying these limitations into a practical, comprehensive model.
why_interesting: This work provides essential theoretical tools for designing and benchmarking quantum optical devices, directly impacting the feasibility of quantum sensing and communication protocols that rely on non-classical light.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Noise sources in squeezing experiments. A generic squeezing measurement consists of a squeezer, a mixer, and a detector. Each section has different noise contributions that can limit the total measured squeezing. Red circle on the left represents vacuum noise in phase space, which is transformed by the squeezer into the squeezed ellipse shown in the mixer section, which after detection is shown by the reduced noise on the electronic spectrum analyzer. 𝜒(2) nonlinear sections are depicted within the squeezer for concreteness - some squeezers instead utilize 𝜒(3) nonlinear sections. The squares with diagonal slashes represent the effective beamsplitters that model how many processes...

Fig. 3. Effects of External and Internal Loss. (a) External loss - loss after a squeezer introduces vacuum fluctuations, which reduces the squeezing as shown in the phase space diagrams. (b) Effect of external loss on squeezed variance (equation (11)), for input squeezing of 5 dB (blue) and 10 dB (red), with dotted lines representing simplified model of equation (12). (c) Internal Loss model of a lossy nonlinear region as a series of infinitesimal nonlinear regions and loss regions. (d) Squeezing evolution with length for two gain rates (𝑔= 12 dB/cm red and 𝑔= 27 dB/cm blue) and fixed loss rate 𝛼= 10 dB/m for a nonresonant squeezer (equation (16). Fig. 6. Fundamental bounds on squeezed light generation. Plotted as a function of gain rate g, for different kinds of squeezers. Light green shaded region represents the feasible squeezing region, for all possible squeezer designs (nonlinear length, resonance, etc). Large dotted lines show noise bounds, for all possible squeezer designs. Solid green and faint dotted lines show the total expected squeezing and the gain-dependent noise limitations for specific squeezer designs. Fig. 7. Squeezed light detection. (a) Balanced homodyne detection mixes the squeezed signal (light red) with a strong local oscillator reference beam (red) and detects the difference photocurrent out of two photodetectors. (b) Parametric homodyne detection mixes the squeezed signal (light red) with a strong pump (blue) and detects the amplified signal. (c) Contributions to detection noise, in decibel shot-noise units, as a function of detected power.
